metadata.career_relevance: Every external call must have a timeout. Without timeouts: one slow service holds your threads forever. @TimeLimiter + CompletableFuture gives you control over maximum wait time. The fallback strategy determines user experience: static default, cached data, degraded mode, or queue-for-retry.

hook.what_you_will_build: Add @TimeLimiter to bound external call duration, choose the right fallback strategy for each criticality level, and combine with @CircuitBreaker.

visual_aid.description: Timeline: external call starts at T=0. Without timeout: thread blocked until T=30s (server timeout). With @TimeLimiter(2s): at T=2s, TimeoutException thrown, fallback called at T=2.001s. Thread freed. Response time: 2.1s vs 30s. Fallback options shown as 4 tiers: static default (fastest), cached (fast), degraded (medium), fail fast (surfaces error).
visual_aid.alt_text: Timeout comparison: 30s blocked thread vs 2s timeout + immediate fallback

worked_example.scenario: Flipkart recommendation engine with 2-second timeout. Fallback returns cached bestsellers.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. @TimeLimiter(2s) + @CircuitBreaker on getRecommendations()
2. Fallback: return cached bestsellers list
3. Test: mock that takes 3 seconds → verify timeout fires, fallback returns bestsellers
worked_example.expected_output:
# Without timeout: 30 second wait → thread blocked
# With @TimeLimiter(2s): after 2 seconds → cachedFallback() → bestsellers returned
things_to_notice: @TimeLimiter REQUIRES CompletableFuture return type — unlike @CircuitBreaker and @Retry.
cancelRunningFuture=true: sends interruption to the running async thread. Without this, the thread keeps running even after timeout (wasting resources).
Fallback also returns CompletableFuture: matching return type rule applies.
Correct stacking: @TimeLimiter (outer) → @CircuitBreaker (inner). CB records 1 timeout failure.

industry_examples.use_case: 2-second timeout on recommendation calls. Fallback: cached bestsellers. Product page loads in 2.1s instead of 30s+ when engine is slow.
Bank account verification API: 3-second timeout. Fallback: mark as 'pending verification', allow payment, verify asynchronously. Payments not blocked by slow bank APIs.

applications.interview_relevance: 'Why must every external call have a timeout?' → Without a timeout, a slow external service holds your thread indefinitely. Thread pool exhausts → your entire service degrades. Timeout + fallback: fails fast, frees the thread immediately.
applications.real_world_workflow: Set timeouts at multiple levels: HTTP client (connection + read), @TimeLimiter (application). Use @CircuitBreaker with timeout failures to detect patterns.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: @TimeLimiter on a method that doesn't return CompletableFuture — it silently doesn't work.
common_gaps.remediation: @TimeLimiter is designed for async operations — it needs CompletableFuture to cancel the future on timeout. Without it: the annotation is ignored.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Method runs longer than timeoutDuration with no TimeoutException thrown

key_takeaways: @TimeLimiter: bounds max duration of external call
cancelRunningFuture=true: interrupts async thread on timeout
5 fallback strategies: static, cached, degraded, fail-fast, queue
Connection timeout (TCP) vs read timeout (server response) vs @TimeLimiter (application level)

self_check_questions.question: What three timeout levels should a production HTTP call have?
self_check_questions.answer: 1. Connection timeout (HTTP client): max time to establish TCP connection (2-3s). 2. Read timeout (HTTP client): max time to receive server response after connection (5-10s). 3. @TimeLimiter (application): application-level max duration, feeds into circuit breaker failure tracking. Each layer catches different failure modes.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Final Module 19 topic: Spring Cloud Gateway with resilience — applying all four patterns at the gateway level to protect all downstream services.
